title: The practice of celebrating yourself without needing external validation
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
I didn't know I was doing it wrong

I spent years waiting for someone else to tell me I was okay. A boss. A partner. A parent. A stranger on the internet. Anyone. I'd do something decent, something I was proud of, and then I'd wait. I'd hold my breath. I'd scan the room for a nod, a smile, a word. And when it didn't come, I'd feel the ground fall out from under me. That's not dramatic. That's what happens when your nervous system learns that safety comes from outside you. Here's the thing. I didn't even know I was doing it. I thought I was just being normal. I thought everyone needed someone to say "good job" before they could feel good. But that's not normal. That's a survival strategy. And it's a brittle one.

Bessel van der Kolk has spent decades showing us how trauma lives in the body, not just in the memory. He talks about how we get stuck in patterns of seeking approval because approval once meant survival. If the people who fed you and kept you safe only smiled when you performed, performed perfectly, performed quietly, performed whatever they needed, then your brain wired itself to chase that smile. That's not weakness. That's adaptation. But the adaptation outlives the threat. And then you're an adult, standing in your own kitchen, waiting for someone to give you permission to feel proud of yourself. That's exhausting. And it's not your fault.

But here's what I've learned. You can change it. Not by force. Not by gritting your teeth and declaring yourself worthy. That never worked for me. It felt fake. It felt like I was lying. What worked was slower. It was quieter. And it involved actually practicing something I had no idea how to do.

The problem with external validation

Look, I'm not saying external validation is bad. It's not. It feels good when someone sees you. It feels good when a friend says "I'm proud of you" or a colleague says "that was really well done." That's connection. That's human. The problem isn't that it feels good. The problem is when you can't feel good without it. When your internal state is entirely dependent on someone else's response. That's not connection. That's dependency. And dependency on other people's moods and availability and attention is a very shaky foundation for a life.

Stephen Porges talks about this through the lens of polyvagal theory. He explains that our nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety or danger. When we rely on external validation, we're outsourcing our regulation to other people. We're saying "I can only feel safe if you signal safety to me." And that's a problem because other people are unpredictable. They're busy. They're distracted. They're dealing with their own nervous systems. They won't always be there to tell you you're okay. And when they're not, you crash. You doubt. You spiral. You think something is wrong with you. Nothing is wrong with you. You just never learned how to do it yourself.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link). It helped me understand that my need for external approval wasn't a character flaw. It was a response to what happened to me. That shifted something. It made the problem less shameful and more solvable.

What celebrating yourself actually looks like

I used to think celebrating yourself meant standing in front of a mirror and saying affirmations. I tried it. It felt awful. It felt like I was pretending. So I stopped. And then I tried something different. Something smaller. Something that actually worked.

Here's what I did. I started noticing when I did something that was hard for me. Not something that was hard for everyone. Something that was hard for me specifically. Like sending an email I was nervous about. Like setting a boundary with someone I loved. Like finishing a task I'd been avoiding for weeks. And instead of waiting for someone to notice, I just paused. I took a breath. And I said to myself, inside my head, "That was hard. I did it." That's it. No fanfare. No fireworks. Just a moment of acknowledgment. Wild, right? That such a small thing could shift something. But it did.

Because here's the thing about your nervous system. It learns through repetition. Every time you pause and acknowledge yourself, you're building a new pathway. You're teaching your brain that you are a source of safety. You're teaching your body that it doesn't need to wait for someone else to signal that you're okay. You can signal it yourself. Deb Dana calls this "glimmering" - actively noticing moments of safety and connection. But you can also glimmer for yourself. You can notice your own competence. Your own effort. Your own growth. And that's not narcissism. That's regulation.

I started doing this with really small things. I'd make my bed and think "I did that." I'd drink water and think "that's good for me." I'd finish a work project and think "I completed that." It felt ridiculous at first. It felt like I was patting myself on the back for breathing. But I kept doing it. And over time, something shifted. I started to feel a small warmth inside when I acknowledged myself. Not a big rush. Not a dopamine hit. Just a quiet sense of "I'm okay." And that was enough.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). It helped me understand that I didn't need to perform for my own approval. I just needed to show up and notice what was already true.

The resistance you'll feel (and why it's normal)

I'm going to be honest with you. When you start practicing this, you will feel resistance. Your brain will tell you it's stupid. It will tell you you're being arrogant. It will tell you that you're just pretending and it doesn't count because no one else saw it. Stay with me here. That resistance is not a sign that you're doing it wrong. It's a sign that you're doing it right. Your nervous system is used to the old pattern. It's used to waiting. It's used to scanning. It's used to depending on others. When you try to do it yourself, your brain sounds an alarm. It says "this is not how we do things. This is not safe. We need someone else to confirm." That alarm is just a habit. It's not the truth.

Peter Levine talks about the importance of pendulation in healing. Moving between discomfort and safety, between the old pattern and the new one. You don't have to be perfect at this. You don't have to believe it fully. You just have to try it. And when the resistance comes, you can notice it. You can say "oh, there's that voice telling me this doesn't count." And then you can do it anyway. You can acknowledge yourself anyway. That's the practice. That's the return.

I remember the first time I tried to celebrate something genuinely meaningful to me. I had finished a project I'd been working on for months. It was hard. It required me to face things I didn't want to face. And I finished it. I sat in my chair and I felt the urge to text someone, to post about it, to get some kind of external hit. And I didn't. I just sat there. I put my hand on my chest. And I said "I'm proud of you." I cried. Not because I was sad. Because I had never said that to myself before. And it mattered. It mattered more than any comment or like or congratulatory text could have mattered. Because it was mine. It came from me. And I couldn't lose it.

How to build the practice

If you want to try this, here's what I'd suggest. Start small. Start with things that feel almost too small to count. Making your bed. Brushing your teeth. Getting out of bed when you didn't want to. Sending a text you were avoiding. Taking a shower. Eating a meal. These are not trivial. These are acts of survival. And they deserve acknowledgment.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did you feel a little dismissive? Did you think "that's too small, that doesn't count"? That's the voice I'm talking about. That's the voice that says you have to earn your own approval. You don't. You just have to show up.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). It gave me concrete tools for building this kind of internal regulation. Tools that didn't require me to believe anything I didn't believe. Just practices I could do.

And if you find yourself struggling with the parts of you that don't want to celebrate yourself, the parts that think you don't deserve it, the parts that are scared of being seen even by yourself, that's okay. A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). It helped me understand that even the parts that resist are trying to protect me. They're not bad. They're just scared.

The practice of celebrating yourself without external validation is not about becoming self-absorbed. It's about becoming self-connected. It's about building a relationship with yourself where you are a source of acknowledgment, not just a consumer of other people's approval. It's about returning to yourself. Returning to your own body. Returning to your own sense of okayness. And it's hard. It's really hard. But it's also the most freeing thing I've ever done.

You don't have to wait for someone else to tell you that you matter. You can tell yourself. And you can mean it. That's the practice. That's the return.

Healing Journey Resources

  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many.
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection.
  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system.
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate.
